sql-server.description: Legacy enterprise SQL Server — PascalCase with occasional 'tbl' prefixes
sql-server.notes: Legacy systems sometimes have inconsistent naming — some PascalCase, some with underscores. Mix it up slightly for realism.

postgresql.description: Modern application databases — clean snake_case throughout
postgresql.notes: Well-organized, modern conventions. Consistent naming throughout.

mysql.description: Web/digital application databases — lowercase snake_case, no schemas
mysql.schema_style: No schemas (or single default schema per app)
mysql.notes: Simple, flat structure. No deep schema hierarchy.

snowflake.description: Analytics warehouse — UPPERCASE with star schema patterns
snowflake.schema_style: UPPERCASE analytical layers: RAW, STAGING, ANALYTICS, RISK, COMPLIANCE
snowflake.table_style: UPPERCASE_SNAKE_CASE, prefixed: DIM_, FACT_, STG_ for star schema
snowflake.notes: Star schema: fact tables have FACT_ prefix, dimension tables have DIM_ prefix. Staging tables have STG_ prefix.

databricks.description: Data lakehouse — medallion architecture with snake_case
databricks.notes: Bronze = raw ingested data (wide, minimal transformation). Silver = cleaned, typed. Gold = business-ready aggregates. Retired/archived datasets go to bronze.

fabric.description: Corporate BI platform — PascalCase with star schema for reporting
fabric.schema_style: PascalCase domain names: FinanceReporting, HRAnalytics, ITGovernance
fabric.table_style: PascalCase, prefixed: Dim, Fact for star schema
fabric.notes: Star schema oriented for Power BI consumption. Dimension and fact table pattern.

oracle.description: Legacy ERP — UPPERCASE, abbreviated names, old-school conventions
oracle.schema_style: UPPERCASE abbreviated: MASREPH, LEASE_MGMT, FIN_OPS, INS_SYS
oracle.table_style: UPPERCASE abbreviated, max 30 chars: CUST_MSTR, CNTRCT_DTL, TXN_HDR
oracle.column_style: UPPERCASE abbreviated: CUST_ID, TXN_DT, AMT, STAT_CD
oracle.notes: 30-char limit on identifiers. Heavy abbreviation. Feels like a 20-year-old ERP. Common abbreviations: MSTR=Master, DTL=Detail, HDR=Header, TXN=Transaction, CNTRCT=Contract, CUST=Customer, ACCT=Account, AMT=Amount, DT=Date, CD=Code, NM=Name, NBR=Number, STAT=Status, ADDR=Address, DESC=Description.

mongodb.description: Document store — camelCase, nested documents instead of joins
mongodb.notes: NO table splitting. Instead, embed related data as nested objects/arrays. Design for read patterns, not normalization. Include nested structures where a relational DB would join.
